topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The turpentine fumes were suddenly too sharp, stinging my nostrils until I had to physically recoil. I stared at the canvas, the vibrant swirl of crimson and gold mocking me from the stretched linen. My breath hitched, a shallow, rapid thing in my chest. It wasn't the composition that bothered me; it was the line work, a deliberate, stark black etching running through the center of my most intricate depiction of a stormy sea. A flash of recognition, cold and sharp, pierced through my usual artistic haze. Someone had marked it. Someone had claimed it. My jaw tightened until my teeth ached. I snatched a brush, the bristles trembling violently, and scraped a thick, ugly streak across the painted waves, the sound of the scraping a ragged expulsion of trapped air.

[story 2]

I paced the studio floor like a caged animal. Every shadow seemed to stretch and distort, amplifying the thrumming anxiety beneath my skin. When I finally turned back to the easel, the sight made my stomach clench into a tight knot. The delicate rendering of the cityscape, the very essence of my latest piece, was overlaid with a precise, almost surgical tattoo. It was a signature, or perhaps a claim. A low growl escaped my throat, a sound I barely recognized. I slammed my fist onto the nearby wooden workbench, the impact jarring my arm. I needed to know who dared to deface my vision, who thought they had the right to imprint their mark onto my creation. My hands shook so badly I had to grip the edge of the frame just to keep them steady.

[story 3]

Silence descended, heavy and suffocating, broken only by the frantic drumming of my pulse against my ribs. I leaned in close, my reflection swimming in the polished surface of the varnish. There it was. A deliberate, intricate design woven into the very fabric of my portrait—a symbol that felt alien, invasive. A deep, burning heat rose from my core, making my skin prickle. I backed away slowly, my movements jerky and uncontrolled. I couldn't look away. My eyes locked onto the mark, and a profound sense of violation washed over me, leaving a bitter, metallic taste in my mouth. I grabbed a rag, not to clean, but to scrub furiously at the surface, trying to erase the intrusion, to wipe away the audacity of the intrusion.
